name: Life Design
subtitle: Level 5 · Owner · Owner Performance
whatThisIs:
Life Design is where the journey comes full circle. You built a business that can run without you. Now the question is: what do you want to do with that freedom?

This is not about retirement. It is about intentional living. The business serves your life, not the other way around. You choose your level of involvement based on what energises you, not what the business demands. You design your days, your years, and your legacy around what matters most to you.

This playbook is for owners who have created something bigger than themselves and now want to live deliberately within that success.

broken.label: When this is broken
broken.content: Success feels hollow. You built something remarkable but you are not sure why. Your calendar is full of things you should do rather than things you want to do. You have financial freedom but no clear vision of what that freedom is for. The business runs itself but you still feel trapped by expectations, your own and others'.

ok.label: When this is OK
ok.content: You have more freedom than most but you have not fully embraced it. Some weeks you feel in control of your time, others you get pulled back into old patterns. You know what you do not want but you are still figuring out what you do want. The infrastructure for independence is there but your mindset has not fully shifted to use it.

working.label: When this is working
working.content: Your life is designed by intention, not accident. You are clear on what brings you energy and what drains it. Your involvement in the business comes from choice, not obligation. You have structured your time, relationships, and commitments around what truly matters to you. You feel fully aligned between who you are and how you spend your days.

thingsToThinkAbout: What does your ideal Tuesday look like? Not your ideal vacation or retirement, your ideal working Tuesday. How involved are you in the business? What time do you start? What brings you energy? Design from the day up, not the year down.

If money was no object, what would you do differently? You have built the wealth. Now what? Many owners discover that the habits that built the business no longer serve the life they want. Security thinking needs to become abundance thinking.

Where do you add the most unique value, in the business and beyond? At this level, your time is your most precious resource. You can afford to be ruthless about where you spend it. What can only you do? What lights you up? What would the world lose if you did not do it?

What legacy do you want to leave? Not the business legacy, that is running without you. The personal legacy. How do you want to be remembered? What impact do you want to have on the people around you? What would make you proud to look back on this chapter?

How do you want to be needed? Most owners struggle with not being needed by the business anymore. But being needed is different from being wanted. How do you want to contribute? Where do you want people to seek your wisdom, experience, or involvement?
actionsIntro: When you open this playbook in the Profit System, you will find:
quickWins: This week: Block out one day next week where you do nothing business-related unless it genuinely excites you. Notice what that feels like.
This week: List three things you are currently involved in out of habit or obligation, not choice. Pick one to delegate or eliminate.
This week: Write down your ideal average day five years from now. Include where you are, what you are doing, who you are with, and how you feel.

detailSections.title: What to Do
detailSections.content:
Map out your ideal life across six dimensions: work, relationships, health, finances, contribution, and adventure. For each dimension, describe what "great" looks like in 3 years and 10 years. Be specific - not "be healthy" but "run a half marathon, sleep 7 hours a night, eat well 5 days a week." This is your personal strategic plan, and the business exists to fund it.

Most owners build their life around the business. This reverses it. You define the life first, then check whether the business is designed to deliver it. If there is a gap, that gap becomes your strategic priority.

detailSections.title: Why It Matters
detailSections.content:
A business without a life vision behind it becomes a trap. You hit revenue targets and still feel empty because you never defined what the money was for. Dan Sullivan calls this "the gap" - measuring yourself against an undefined ideal instead of concrete progress. The canvas gives you something real to measure against.

Tim Ferriss asks: "What would you do with $10 million in the bank?" Most owners cannot answer clearly. That lack of clarity means the business drifts toward "more" instead of "enough." Enough is the most powerful number in business because it lets you make decisions from abundance, not anxiety.

detailSections.title: How to Use This
detailSections.content:
Set aside 90 minutes with no distractions. Work through each dimension honestly. Share it with your coach - the conversation about gaps between current reality and your vision is where the real value sits.

---

detailSections.title: Life Dimensions
detailSections.content:
### Work & Career
- [ ] **Define your ideal working week** - How many hours? Which days? What type of work? Be honest about what energises you versus what you do out of habit.
- [ ] **Describe your ideal role in the business** - Are you the operator, the strategist, the chair, or gone entirely? There is no wrong answer, but there must be an answer.
- [ ] ⚡ Quick Win: **Write down 3 things you do in the business that you would happily never do again** - This is your first delegation shortlist.

### Relationships & Family
- [ ] **Describe your ideal family life** - How present are you? What activities do you share? What milestones do you refuse to miss?
- [ ] **Assess your key relationships honestly** - Partner, children, friends, parents. Which are thriving? Which are on autopilot? Which need repair?
- [ ] **Define non-negotiable commitments** - School runs, date nights, family holidays. Put them in the calendar before business commitments.

### Health & Energy
- [ ] **Set specific health targets** - Weight, fitness level, sleep quality, stress management. Vague goals produce vague results.
- [ ] **Identify your current health risks** - What are you ignoring? Skipped check-ups, poor sleep, no exercise, too much alcohol. Name it honestly.
- [ ] ⚡ Quick Win: **Book any overdue health appointments this week** - GP check-up, dentist, optician. Takes 5 minutes to book.

### Finances & Wealth
- [ ] **Define your "enough" number** - What annual passive income would mean you never need to work again? Write it down. This drives your wealth-building strategy (see [[5.4 Wealth Building]]).
- [ ] **Map where you are today versus that number** - No judgement, just clarity. The gap is your financial roadmap.

### Contribution & Legacy
- [ ] **Define the impact you want to have beyond profit** - Mentoring, charity, community, industry change. What matters to you when the business chapter ends?
- [ ] **Identify one contribution you could start now** - You do not need to wait until you are "successful enough." Start small.

### Adventure & Growth
- [ ] **List experiences you want to have** - Travel, learning, creative projects, challenges. Things that make life feel full, not just productive.
- [ ] **Schedule one adventure in the next 90 days** - Not "someday." A specific trip, experience, or challenge with a date attached.

---

detailSections.title: Expected Outcome
detailSections.content:
- A written personal vision across all six life dimensions
- Clear picture of gaps between current reality and ideal future
- Specific, measurable targets rather than vague aspirations
- A foundation that every business decision can be tested against: "Does this move me toward or away from my life vision?"
- Motivation grounded in something deeper than revenue targets

detailSections.title: Coach Notes
detailSections.content:
This is often an emotional session. Many owners have not thought about what they actually want in years. They have been so focused on the business that personal vision has atrophied. Give them space and time.

Watch for two traps. First, the owner who designs a vision that requires selling the business tomorrow - help them build a realistic timeline. Second, the owner who cannot think beyond the business at all - they have merged identity with the company and need help separating the two. The question "Who are you without the business?" is uncomfortable but essential.

Revisit the canvas quarterly. Life vision is not static - it evolves as circumstances change. The value is in the regular check-in, not the initial exercise.

detailSections.title: What to Do
detailSections.content:
Track your energy levels across every activity in your week, then redesign your commitments around what gives you energy and what drains it. For one week, rate every meeting, task, and interaction on a simple scale: gives energy (+), neutral (0), or drains energy (-). At the end of the week, sort the results and make decisions about what stays, what goes, and what changes.

At Level 5, managing energy matters more than managing time. You have probably already optimised your calendar. The next breakthrough comes from optimising what fills it.

detailSections.title: Why It Matters
detailSections.content:
Jim Loehr's research on high performers shows that energy, not time, is the fundamental currency of performance. A business owner running on depleted energy makes worse decisions, damages relationships, and cannot think strategically. You can have 4 free hours in your diary, but if you arrive at them exhausted from energy-draining activities, those hours are wasted.

Most owners tolerate energy drains because they feel obligated - "That is just part of running a business." At Level 5, you have the resources and team to eliminate or delegate most of those drains. The question is whether you give yourself permission to do it.

detailSections.title: How to Use This
detailSections.content:
Carry a simple tracking sheet (or use notes on your phone) for one full working week. Log every activity and rate its energy impact. Then sit down with the data and make three lists: amplify, redesign, or eliminate.

---

detailSections.title: Week-Long Energy Tracking
detailSections.content:
- [ ] **Create a simple energy log** - Three columns: Activity, Duration, Energy Impact (+, 0, -). Keep it with you all week.
- [ ] ⚡ Quick Win: **Rate yesterday's activities from memory right now** - You will immediately spot patterns you already know but have not acted on.
- [ ] **Log every activity for 5 full working days** - Meetings, calls, admin, creative work, travel, social interactions, even lunch. Miss nothing.
- [ ] **Note the time of day for each entry** - Some activities drain you at 8am but energise you at 2pm. Time context matters.
- [ ] **Include personal commitments too** - Exercise, family time, hobbies, social events. Energy does not reset at 5pm.

detailSections.title: Analysis (End of Week)
detailSections.content:
- [ ] **Sort all activities into three columns: Energisers, Neutral, and Drains** - Count them. Most owners discover 30-40% of their week is spent on draining activities.
- [ ] **For each energy drain, ask: Can this be eliminated, delegated, or redesigned?** - Not "Should I keep doing this?" but "Is there any way I do not have to?"
- [ ] **Identify your peak energy windows** - When during the day are you at your best? Your most important work should live in those windows, not your most draining.
- [ ] **Look for hidden drains** - Some activities seem fine but leave you flat. Certain people, meeting formats, or task types that subtly deplete you over time.
- [ ] **Identify energy amplifiers you are underusing** - Activities that leave you energised but rarely make it into your week. Exercise, creative work, mentoring, strategic thinking.

detailSections.title: Redesign Commitments
detailSections.content:
- [ ] **Move your top 3 energisers into your weekly schedule as non-negotiables** - Protect them the way you protect client meetings.
- [ ] **Eliminate or delegate your top 3 energy drains this month** - Start with the easiest one to remove. Build momentum.
- [ ] **Restructure your day around energy patterns** - High-energy work in peak windows. Admin and routine tasks in low-energy slots. Never put draining meetings before important work.
- [ ] ⚡ Quick Win: **Cancel or shorten one recurring meeting that drains you** - You probably already know which one.

---

detailSections.title: Expected Outcome
detailSections.content:
- Clear map of what gives and takes energy across your entire week
- At least 3 energy drains eliminated or delegated within 30 days
- Daily schedule restructured around natural energy patterns
- More hours spent in flow states on work that energises you
- Better decisions because you are making them with full energy, not fumes

detailSections.title: Coach Notes
detailSections.content:
This action often produces an "I already knew that" reaction. The owner knows what drains them. The value is in making it visible and giving them permission to act on it. Many owners feel guilty about eliminating things that drain them, especially if those things involve people or long-standing commitments.

Push back gently on "I cannot delegate that." At Level 5, the owner has a team. If they are still doing energy-draining work that someone else could handle, the issue is trust or control, not capability.

Re-run the energy audit every quarter. What drains you changes as the business evolves. New drains creep in and old energisers get crowded out if you are not watching.

detailSections.title: What to Do
detailSections.content:
Define your ideal relationship with the business: how much time you spend in it, in what capacity, and on what timeline you want to get there. Turn "I should be involved in everything" into "I choose to be involved in these specific areas because they align with my strengths and my life vision."

Map every area of the business where you are currently involved, then decide deliberately which to keep, which to hand over, and which to phase out. Set a 12-month target for your ideal involvement level and work backward to a quarterly plan.

detailSections.title: Why It Matters
detailSections.content:
Most owners' involvement in their business is accidental. It is the accumulation of years of being the only person who could do things, combined with habits that never got questioned. At Level 5, you have the team and systems to choose. But choice requires a deliberate design - otherwise you drift back into doing everything.

Michael Gerber's insight still applies: the owner should work on the business, not in it. But at Level 5, the question is sharper. It is not just "on versus in" but "which parts of 'on' do I want to keep?" Some owners love strategy. Some love product. Some love client relationships. The answer is personal, and it should be intentional.

detailSections.title: How to Use This
detailSections.content:
Work through the involvement mapping first to see reality clearly. Then design your ideal involvement model and build a transition plan to get there.

---

detailSections.title: Current Involvement Map
detailSections.content:
- [ ] **List every recurring commitment you have in the business** - Meetings, reviews, approvals, calls, decisions. Everything that shows up in your calendar or inbox regularly.
- [ ] **For each commitment, note: hours per week, why you are involved, and whether anyone else could do it** - Be brutally honest about the "why." If the answer is "because I always have," that is not a reason.
- [ ] **Calculate your total weekly hours in the business** - Include the invisible hours: thinking about problems at dinner, checking emails on weekends, calls during holidays.
- [ ] **Categorise each activity: Strategic (only I can do this), Transitional (I am training someone), or Legacy (I am still doing this out of habit)** - Most owners find 40-60% of their time falls in the Legacy category.

detailSections.title: Design Your Ideal Involvement
detailSections.content:
- [ ] **Define your ideal weekly hours** - Be specific. Not "less" but a number. 20 hours? 10? 5? Match this to your Life Vision Canvas.
- [ ] **Choose your ideal areas of involvement** - Where do you add the most value and get the most energy? Strategy, product, key relationships, culture? Pick no more than 3.
- [ ] **Define what "done" looks like for areas you are leaving** - Who takes over? What authority do they need? What does the handover process look like?
- [ ] ⚡ Quick Win: **Identify one meeting you attend out of habit and stop attending this week** - Tell the team you trust them to handle it. See what happens.

detailSections.title: Transition Plan
detailSections.content:
- [ ] **Set a 12-month target for your involvement level** - Where do you want to be in one year? Write it down as a specific weekly schedule.
- [ ] **Break it into quarterly milestones** - Q1: hand over X. Q2: step back from Y. Q3: delegate Z. Q4: operating at target level.
- [ ] **Identify capability gaps** - Where does the team need development before you can step back? Start building that capability now, not when you are ready to leave.
- [ ] **Build check-in rhythms that replace your presence** - Weekly dashboards, monthly reviews, quarterly strategy sessions. You stay informed without being involved in execution.
- [ ] **Communicate the plan to your leadership team** - They need to know this is intentional, not abandonment. Frame it as trust and growth for them, not just freedom for you.

---

detailSections.title: Expected Outcome
detailSections.content:
- Written map of current involvement with honest assessment of what is necessary versus habitual
- Clear definition of ideal involvement across no more than 3 focus areas
- 12-month transition plan with quarterly milestones
- Team prepared and capable of operating with less owner involvement
- Owner spending time on what they choose, not what they feel they should

detailSections.title: Coach Notes
detailSections.content:
This is one of the most identity-challenging actions in the entire system. Many owners intellectually want to step back but emotionally struggle because the business is who they are. If a client resists, explore the identity question: "If you are not the person running this business day-to-day, who are you?"

Watch for the owner who designs an ideal involvement model that is still 50 hours a week but just in "different" areas. That is not redesign, that is rearranging. Challenge them on whether they are genuinely reducing or just reshuffling.

The transition plan must include capability building for the team. Owners who step back without preparing their people create a vacuum that pulls them right back in within weeks.

detailSections.title: What to Do
detailSections.content:
Design systems and boundaries that protect your personal freedom while maintaining your responsibilities and relationships. Freedom without structure becomes chaos - you end up reactive, guilty, and no more in control than before. This action builds the scaffolding that makes genuine freedom sustainable.

Audit where freedom breaks down in practice, then build specific structures - boundaries, systems, communication protocols, and defaults - that protect your time, energy, and choices.

detailSections.title: Why It Matters
detailSections.content:
Many owners achieve financial success but never achieve personal freedom because they lack the structure to sustain it. They take a holiday and check email every hour. They delegate a decision then override it. They set a boundary then break it at the first test. The missing piece is not willpower - it is systems.

Greg McKeown's "Effortless" principle applies here: make the right behaviour the default and the wrong behaviour require effort. If checking email on holiday is easy and not checking requires discipline, you will check. If your phone is locked in the hotel safe and your team knows not to call unless it is a genuine emergency, you will not. Structure beats willpower every time.

detailSections.title: How to Use This
detailSections.content:
Work through this checklist and implement the structures that apply to your situation. Not every item will be relevant, but most will. The goal is to build a set of defaults that protect freedom automatically.

---

detailSections.title: Boundary Design
detailSections.content:
- [ ] **Define your working hours and communicate them clearly** - Not aspirational hours. Actual hours you will work, starting this week. Tell your team, your clients, and your family.
- [ ] **Create an "unavailable" protocol** - What happens when someone needs you during non-work hours? Who do they contact instead? What counts as a genuine emergency? Write it down and share it.
- [ ] **Set device boundaries** - When does your phone go into Do Not Disturb? Where does your laptop stay on weekends? Physical separation from devices is more effective than willpower.
- [ ] ⚡ Quick Win: **Turn off all non-essential notifications on your phone today** - Email, Slack, social media. Keep calls and texts from key people. Everything else can wait.

detailSections.title: Communication Systems
detailSections.content:
- [ ] **Build an async-first communication culture** - Not everything needs a call or instant response. Train your team to use written updates, recorded videos, and shared dashboards instead of pulling you into conversations.
- [ ] **Set response time expectations** - "I check messages twice a day and respond within 24 hours" is reasonable. Let people know so they stop expecting instant replies.
- [ ] **Create a weekly update system** - One document or dashboard that tells you everything you need to know without attending meetings. If the update raises a concern, schedule a focused conversation.
- [ ] **Designate an operational proxy** - One person who can make 90% of decisions without you. Not because you are absent, but because you are choosing to focus elsewhere.

detailSections.title: Decision Guardrails
detailSections.content:
- [ ] **Define which decisions require your input** - Be specific. Spending over a certain threshold, hiring, strategy changes, key client issues. Everything else is delegated.
- [ ] **Create decision criteria documents** - For recurring decisions, write down how you would make the call. Give the criteria to your team so they can decide without you.
- [ ] **Accept that delegated decisions will sometimes be different from yours** - Different is not wrong. If the outcome is acceptable, the decision was good enough. Perfection is the enemy of freedom.

detailSections.title: Freedom Rituals
detailSections.content:
- [ ] **Schedule a quarterly "life audit" week** - One week per quarter where you step back from the business entirely. Review your Life Vision Canvas, check your involvement levels, reset priorities.
- [ ] **Protect transition time** - 30 minutes between work and personal time to decompress. Walk, exercise, meditate. Do not go straight from a stressful call into family dinner.
- [ ] ⚡ Quick Win: **Block one full day this month with no work commitments** - Not a holiday. A day where you do whatever you want with no business obligations. See how it feels.

---

detailSections.title: Expected Outcome
detailSections.content:
- Clear, communicated boundaries around working hours and availability
- Systems that provide business visibility without requiring constant involvement
- Team equipped to operate independently within defined guardrails
- Sustainable freedom that does not create guilt, anxiety, or business risk
- A default mode that protects personal time rather than sacrificing it

detailSections.title: Coach Notes
detailSections.content:
The biggest obstacle here is usually guilt. Owners feel guilty about being unavailable, about not responding immediately, about taking time for themselves. Help them reframe: boundaries are not selfish, they are sustainable. An owner who burns out serves nobody.

Test boundaries gradually. Do not go from 24/7 availability to complete disconnection overnight. Start with one boundary (no email after 7pm) and hold it for a month. Once that feels normal, add another.

If a client's boundaries keep getting broken, investigate why. Is the team not capable enough? Is the client unable to let go of control? Is there a genuine operational gap? The fix depends on the cause. Structure without capability underneath it just creates different problems.
